reasoning: The hypothesis `∀ H : Subgroup G, H = ⊤ ∨ H = ⊥` correctly encodes "G has no proper (nontrivial) subgroups" — i.e., every subgroup is {e} or G — which is the standard Herstein reading (under any other reading, the claim is false or vacuous). The extra `h_nontrivial` hypothesis surfaces an implicit premise: without it, the trivial group satisfies hG vacuously but has order 1, contradicting the conclusion of prime order; any mathematician reading "G is cyclic of prime order" understands G must be nontrivial. This matches the worked-example pattern in the prompt. The conclusion `IsCyclic G ∧ ∃ p, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.card G = p` faithfully encodes "cyclic of order p with p prime", and `Nat.card G = p` with p prime implicitly forces finiteness (since Nat.card is 0 for infinite types). No hypothesis, conclusion, or type is altered beyond surfacing the implicit nontriviality assumption.
